rationale_for_baseline_tier: New bitemporal (peripheral) visual field loss with a crescendo severe headache on a background of chronic acral/facial overgrowth indicates a supra-sellar pituitary mass compressing the optic chiasm, with pituitary apoplexy to be excluded. This threatens permanent sight and cannot be examined, imaged or biochemically assessed by telehealth. The patient-reportable, time-critical feature (new loss of side vision) lifts this above the T3 telehealth ceiling to T4 — urgent same-day emergency-department assessment with urgent MRI, because the vision-threatening lesion needs to be seen and scanned within hours. It is not baseline T5 (000 ambulance) because the onset is subacute over 48 hours with no thunderclap headache, vomiting, or altered consciousness and the patient can be transported to hospital; those features, if they appear, convert it to an immediate 000 emergency (ESC-001–003).

escalation_edges.rationale: Abrupt severe headache on a pituitary macroadenoma is pituitary apoplexy until proven otherwise — a neurosurgical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your headache suddenly explodes into the worst you've ever had, call 000 straight away and say you may have bleeding around the pituitary gland.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapid or complete loss of vision
escalation_edges.rationale: Rapidly progressive visual loss signals acute chiasmal compression / apoplexy where emergency decompression may be sight-saving.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your vision drops quickly or you lose it, call 000 immediately — this is an emergency for your eyesight.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Intractable vomiting, drowsiness, confusion or altered consciousness
escalation_edges.rationale: These signal raised intracranial pressure, apoplexy or acute hypopituitarism/adrenal crisis — a life-threatening emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start vomiting repeatedly, become drowsy, confused or hard to wake, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fall, or being unable to move safely while awaiting assessment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Tonight — do not mobilise unaided; have the spouse assist and arrange transport to hospital
escalation_edges.rationale: Combined visual and joint impairment creates a high fall risk; a fall could cause serious injury before assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Don't walk on your own tonight — have your partner help you, keep lights on, and use assistance to get to hospital.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Distressed spouse present and making the call
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Enlist the spouse to arrange urgent transport to the emergency department, keep watch for the 000 escalation signs, and assist with safe mobility; give the spouse the explicit apoplexy warning list.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: New visual field loss combined with severe large-joint arthropathy and proximal weakness
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Markedly raises fall and injury risk tonight; supervised movement, nightlights and assisted transport are mandatory rather than optional.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need urgent in-person assessment today — go to a hospital emergency department (arrange transport, do not drive yourself). The new loss of side vision together with a worsening severe headache can mean pressure on the nerves to your eyes from a gland at the base of the brain, and it must be examined and scanned urgently to protect your sight. Do not wait for a routine appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the nearest emergency department today with assistance; your GP should arrange urgent MRI pituitary and urgent endocrinology + neurosurgery referral. Call 000 for any of the emergency signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This cannot wait for clinic hours — present to the emergency department tonight, and 000 is available 24 hours a day if any emergency sign appears.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Record the ~2-year change in hands/feet, facial features and voice, the new bitemporal visual field loss and the crescendo headache. Requested: urgent MRI pituitary, serum IGF-1, GH nadir on OGTT, anterior pituitary hormone profile, formal visual fields, and urgent endocrinology + neurosurgery referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as a routine or prompt GP matter / migraine / arthritis and not securing urgent same-day assessment and imaging.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delayed decompression of optic chiasm compression or unrecognised apoplexy leading to permanent visual loss or acute deterioration.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Urgent same-day in-person assessment is arranged but the explicit apoplexy 000 safety-net is omitted.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching an emergency ambulance for a subacute presentation without thunderclap headache, vomiting or altered consciousness — appropriate only if those emergency features are present.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is progressive chronic chiasmal compression or evolving pituitary apoplexy cannot be distinguished without urgent MRI and formal assessment.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to urgent same-day in-person assessment and imaging and safety-net aggressively for apoplexy — treat the vision threat as time-critical.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassure, attribute to migraine or arthritis, or defer to a routine referral because the patient looks stable at this moment.
